title: Bacterial diseases — salmonella, gonorrhoea
examTip: AQA uses these two bacterial diseases to test very different ideas: food safety and vaccination of livestock (Salmonella) versus STIs, antibiotic treatment, and resistance (gonorrhoea). The antibiotic resistance section is especially important — it links to the global health crisis and appears regularly.
introTips: Two diseases to know well: Salmonella (food poisoning) and gonorrhoea (STI). They're very different in how they spread and how they're treated — but both are caused by bacteria, so antibiotics are in the picture for both.

The antibiotic resistance section in this lesson is genuinely important beyond the exam — it's one of the biggest threats in medicine right now. Understanding why finishing a course of antibiotics matters is something you'll actually use.
MRSA and antibiotic-resistant gonorrhoea are both examples of the same underlying problem: bacteria evolving faster than we can develop new drugs.
intro: Food poisoning. STIs. Antibiotic resistance. Three very different-sounding topics — all connected through bacteria. Salmonella lives in raw chicken and eggs; gonorrhoea is a sexually transmitted infection with a growing resistance problem. Understanding both tells you a lot about how bacteria cause disease, how we try to stop them, and why that's getting harder. The antibiotic resistance story isn't just exam content — it's one of the most serious challenges in modern medicine.

keyPoints.heading: Salmonella — food poisoning from bacteria in your food
keyPoints.content: Salmonella is a type of bacteria that causes food poisoning. It's most commonly found in raw or undercooked chicken and eggs, and it can be spread by poor food hygiene — using the same chopping board for raw meat and salad, for example. Once inside your digestive system, Salmonella bacteria reproduce rapidly and release toxins that cause symptoms: fever, vomiting, diarrhoea, and stomach cramps. These usually appear 12–72 hours after eating contaminated food and last a few days. The illness is caused by the toxins, not by the bacteria directly damaging your gut. Prevention focuses on three areas: cooking food thoroughly (heat kills the bacteria), food hygiene (separate raw and cooked food, wash hands, keep surfaces clean), and — importantly for AQA — vaccinating poultry. UK chickens are vaccinated against Salmonella, which dramatically reduced the number of human cases. This is a good example of controlling a disease at the source rather than treating it after the fact.
writeBullets: Salmonella = bacteria that cause food poisoning
Spread by: contaminated food (undercooked chicken, eggs), poor food hygiene
Symptoms: fever, vomiting, diarrhoea, stomach cramps
Caused by toxins released by bacteria (not bacteria directly)
Prevention: cook food thoroughly, food hygiene, vaccinate poultry

keyTerms.def: Bacteria that cause food poisoning; spread through contaminated food (especially chicken and eggs)
Illness caused by eating food contaminated with bacteria or their toxins
A poison released by bacteria that causes symptoms like fever, vomiting, and diarrhoea

keyPoints.memTip: Salmonella: Spread (food), Symptoms (FVDC — Fever, Vomiting, Diarrhoea, Cramps), Source of toxin, Stop it (cook + hygiene + vaccinate poultry). The poultry vaccination point catches students out — make sure it's on your card.

keyPoints.heading: Gonorrhoea — an STI with a growing resistance problem
keyPoints.content: Gonorrhoea is a sexually transmitted infection (STI) caused by the bacterium Neisseria gonorrhoeae. It spreads through sexual contact — unprotected sex with an infected person. Symptoms include a thick yellow or green discharge from the penis or vagina, and pain when urinating — though some people have no symptoms at all, which makes it easier for the infection to spread unknowingly. Historically, gonorrhoea was easily treated with antibiotics. However, antibiotic-resistant strains of Neisseria gonorrhoeae have developed and are now spreading globally, making the infection much harder to treat. Some strains are resistant to nearly all available antibiotics, which is why gonorrhoea is considered a serious public health concern. Prevention focuses on barrier methods — specifically condoms — which physically stop the bacteria passing between individuals during sexual contact.
writeBullets: Caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae bacteria
Spread by sexual contact (STI)
Symptoms: thick yellow/green discharge, pain urinating
Previously treated with antibiotics — antibiotic-resistant strains now a major problem
Prevention: barrier methods (condoms)

keyTerms.def: A sexually transmitted infection caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae bacteria; spread by sexual contact
A strain of bacteria that has evolved resistance to antibiotics, making it harder to treat
A contraceptive (e.g. condom) that physically prevents contact between sexual partners, also preventing STI transmission

keyPoints.memTip: Gonorrhoea: Neisseria gonorrhoeae, spreads by Sex, Symptoms: Discharge + Pain urinating, historically treated with antibiotics but now RESISTANT, prevented by condoms. The resistance point is the one AQA tests — it links directly to the next section.

keyPoints.heading: How antibiotics work — and what they can't do
keyPoints.content: Antibiotics are drugs that kill bacteria or stop bacteria reproducing. Different types of antibiotic work in different ways — some attack the bacterial cell wall, some disrupt the bacterium's ability to make proteins. Penicillin, discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928, was the first widely used antibiotic and saved millions of lives. The critical thing about antibiotics is what they cannot do: they do not work on viruses. Viruses don't have the bacterial structures that antibiotics target. Taking antibiotics for a viral infection (like flu or a cold) does nothing to treat the virus — it just risks helping resistant bacteria develop. This distinction — bacteria vs viruses, antibiotics vs antivirals — is tested repeatedly in AQA papers.
writeBullets: Antibiotics kill bacteria or stop them reproducing
Different antibiotics target different bacteria
Antibiotics do NOT work on viruses — viruses lack the structures antibiotics target
Penicillin = first widely used antibiotic (Fleming, 1928)

keyTerms.def: A drug that kills bacteria or stops them reproducing — does not work on viruses
The first widely used antibiotic, discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928

keyPoints.memTip: Antibiotics = bacteria only. NOT viruses. If you write 'antibiotics treat viruses' in the exam, you lose marks. Connect it to the diseases you know: Salmonella and gonorrhoea → antibiotics can help. Measles and HIV → antibiotics are useless.

keyPoints.heading: Antibiotic resistance — why it's a global crisis
keyPoints.content: Antibiotic resistance happens because of natural selection. Within a population of bacteria, there is genetic variation — random mutations mean some bacteria are slightly less affected by an antibiotic than others. When antibiotics are used, they kill the susceptible bacteria but resistant bacteria survive and reproduce, passing on the resistance. Over many generations, the resistant strain dominates. This is evolution by natural selection, and it happens extremely quickly in bacteria because they reproduce so fast. MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus) is a well-known example — a bacterium that has become resistant to most common antibiotics and is particularly dangerous in hospitals. Antibiotic-resistant gonorrhoea is another. The problem is made worse by two human behaviours: not finishing a course of antibiotics (leaving the most resistant bacteria alive to reproduce) and overprescribing (using antibiotics when they're not needed, giving bacteria more exposure and more chances to develop resistance). There are very few new antibiotics being developed, which is why the World Health Organisation calls this one of the greatest threats to global health.
writeBullets: Antibiotic resistance arises through natural selection
Random mutations → some bacteria less affected by antibiotic
Antibiotic kills susceptible bacteria; resistant bacteria survive and reproduce
Resistant strain eventually dominates — the antibiotic stops working
MRSA = well-known antibiotic-resistant bacterium; dangerous in hospitals
Not finishing a course leaves resistant bacteria alive to reproduce
Overuse and overprescribing accelerates resistance

keyTerms.def: When bacteria evolve to survive exposure to an antibiotic, making it ineffective
The process by which individuals with advantageous traits survive and reproduce, passing those traits on
Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus — a bacterium resistant to most common antibiotics; a major problem in hospitals
A random change in DNA that may confer resistance to an antibiotic in some bacteria

keyPoints.memTip: Resistance = natural selection in fast-forward. One sentence for the exam: 'Random mutations produce resistant bacteria; antibiotics kill susceptible ones; resistant bacteria survive and reproduce, passing on resistance.' Write that sentence, cover it, write it again. It's the whole mechanism in one go.

1.question: A person eats undercooked chicken and develops fever, vomiting, and diarrhoea 24 hours later. What is the most likely cause of their symptoms?
options: Salmonella bacteria releasing toxins in the digestive system
The immune system reacting to uncooked protein in the chicken
A fungal infection from the chicken skin
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — Salmonella bacteria reproduce in the digestive system and release toxins that cause the classic food poisoning symptoms.
1.wrongFeedback: Salmonella is a bacterium, not a virus. Once in the digestive system it reproduces rapidly and releases toxins — and it's those toxins that cause fever, vomiting, and diarrhoea. Remember: bacteria cause disease mainly through toxins.

3.question: Why should you always finish a full course of antibiotics, even if you feel better before the end?
options: To make sure the antibiotic also kills any viruses in your body
Stopping early leaves the most resistant bacteria alive to reproduce, which can lead to resistance developing
Antibiotics only start working after the full course is complete
Stopping early causes an allergic reaction
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right. Feeling better means susceptible bacteria have been killed — but resistant ones may still be present. Stopping early gives them a chance to reproduce and become dominant.
3.wrongFeedback: Finishing the course is about natural selection. When you feel better, the most susceptible bacteria have been killed — but the most resistant ones may still be present in smaller numbers. Stop early and those resistant bacteria reproduce unchallenged, eventually giving you a harder-to-treat infection.

commonMistakes: Saying Salmonella is a virus — it's a bacterium. Bacteria cause disease through toxins; viruses hijack cells. The distinction matters for treatment too.
Forgetting to mention poultry vaccination as a prevention for Salmonella — AQA specifically includes this, and it's not something you'd guess from the other prevention methods.
Writing that antibiotics cure gonorrhoea — historically they did, but antibiotic-resistant strains are now the key issue. AQA expects you to know resistance is a current problem.
Describing antibiotic resistance as 'the person becoming resistant' — it's the bacteria that become resistant, not the person. The bacteria evolve through natural selection.
Saying bacteria 'mutate on purpose' to resist antibiotics — mutations are random. Natural selection then acts on those random mutations. The 'on purpose' idea loses marks.
examTips: For antibiotic resistance, always use the words 'natural selection' — random mutation produces resistant bacteria, antibiotic kills susceptible ones, resistant bacteria survive and reproduce. That's the mechanism.
For gonorrhoea, the resistance angle is what AQA is most interested in — connect it to the broader problem of antibiotic resistance and why finishing a course matters.
For Salmonella, remember all three prevention methods: thorough cooking, food hygiene, AND vaccination of poultry. The third one is the one students miss.
revisionCardBullets: Salmonella: food poisoning | spread by contaminated food (chicken, eggs) | symptoms: fever, vomiting, diarrhoea, cramps
Salmonella prevented by: thorough cooking + food hygiene + vaccinating poultry
Gonorrhoea: STI caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae | symptoms: discharge + pain urinating
Gonorrhoea prevented by barrier methods (condoms); antibiotic-resistant strains now a major problem
Antibiotics kill bacteria or stop them reproducing — do NOT work on viruses
Antibiotic resistance: random mutations → some bacteria survive antibiotic → reproduce → resistant strain dominates
MRSA = methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus — dangerous hospital bacterium resistant to most antibiotics
Not finishing a course of antibiotics → resistant bacteria survive → resistance spreads
Overuse and overprescribing antibiotics accelerates the development of resistance
